coredata.dc:title: Direct chemiluminescence of carbon dots induced by potassium ferricyanide and its analytical application
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2013 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Amjadi, Mohammad
Manzoori, Jamshid L.
Hallaj, Tooba
Sorouraddin, Mohammad H.
coredata.dc:description: The chemiluminescence (CL) of water-soluble fluorescent carbon dots (C-dots) induced by direct chemical oxidation was investigated. C-dots were prepared by solvothermal method and characterized by fluorescence spectra and transmission electron microscopy. It was found that K3Fe(CN)6 could directly oxidize C-dots to produce a relatively intense CL emission. The mechanism of CL generation was investigated based on the fluorescence and CL emission spectra and the effect of radical scavengers on the CL intensity. The inhibitive effect of some metal ions and biologically important molecules on the CL intensity of the system was examined and the potential of the system for the determination of these species at trace levels was studied. In order to evaluate the capability of method to real sample analysis, it was applied to the determination of Cr(VI) and adrenaline in water and injection samples, respectively.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Chemiluminescence
Potassium ferricyanide
Nanomaterials

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Analytical application of the CL system

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM image of the prepared C-dots.

(A) UV–Vis absorption spectrum of C-dots. The inset is the photograph of the C-dots under visible light (left) and under 365nm UV light (right) and (B) fluorescence spectra for C-dots excited at wavelengths of 300–500nm, with increments of 20nm (a–k).

Effect of different oxidants on the CL intensity of C-dots (2.85×10−2 molL−1). All oxidants were in basic medium (NaOH, 5×10−2 molL−1) except Ce(IV), which prepared in sulfuric acid medium (0.2molL−1).
Kinetic curve for C-dot/K3Fe(CN)6 CL system. Conditions: C-dot, 0.285molL−1, K3Fe(CN)6, 10−4 molL−1, NaOH, 0.75molL−1.

(A) CL spectrum of C-dot/K3Fe(CN)6 system, obtained with continuous flow of reagents: K3Fe(CN)6 (2×10−4 molL−1) and NaOH (4molL−1) in one line and C-dot solution (0.1molL−1) in other line; (b) fluorescence spectrum of C-dots, 0.285molL−1, at λ ex =450nm. Inset shows the CL spectra for (a) C-dot/KMnO4 and (b) C-dot/H2O2 system in 1.0molL−1 NaOH solution. (B) Absorption spectrum of K3Fe(CN)6 (5×10−4 molL−1) in 0.75molL−1 NaOH solution (a) before, (b) after addition of 0.095molL−1 C-dot and (c) after addition of 0.19molL−1 C-dot solution.

(A) Effect of the reaction time on the CL intensity. Conditions: C-dot, 0.285molL−1, K3Fe(CN)6, 10−3 molL−1, NaOH, 5×10−2 molL−1; (B) effect of C-dot concentration on the CL intensity. Conditions: the solvothermal reaction time, 12h; other conditions are as in A.

(A) Effect of K3Fe(CN)6 concentration on the CL intensity. Conditions: C-dot, 0.285 molL−1; other conditions are as in Fig. 6B; (B) effect of NaOH concentration Conditions: K3Fe(CN)6, 10−4 molL−1, other conditions are as in A.
Inhibition effect of some organic compounds and metal ions (1.0mgL−1) on the proposed CL system.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: The percentage of quenching was calculated as ΔI/I 0 ×100, where ΔI = I o−I, I and I 0 are the CL intensity in the presence and absence of the added species, respectively.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Analytical performance of the proposed CL system for the determination of some biologically important molecules and metal ions.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: ΔI = I o−I, I o and I are the relative CL intensity in the absence and presence of analytes, respectively, and C is the concentration of analytes.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Determination results for Cr(VI) in real water samples.

head.ce:title: Direct chemiluminescence of carbon dots induced by potassium ferricyanide and its analytical application

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Analytical Chemistry, Faculty of Chemistry, University of Tabriz, Tabriz 5166616471, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The chemiluminescence (CL) of water-soluble fluorescent carbon dots (C-dots) induced by direct chemical oxidation was investigated. C-dots were prepared by solvothermal method and characterized by fluorescence spectra and transmission electron microscopy. It was found that K3Fe(CN)6 could directly oxidize C-dots to produce a relatively intense CL emission. The mechanism of CL generation was investigated based on the fluorescence and CL emission spectra and the effect of radical scavengers on the CL intensity. The inhibitive effect of some metal ions and biologically important molecules on the CL intensity of the system was examined and the potential of the system for the determination of these species at trace levels was studied. In order to evaluate the capability of method to real sample analysis, it was applied to the determination of Cr(VI) and adrenaline in water and injection samples, respectively.

ce:section.ce:para: Luminescent nanoparticles (NPs) have recently attracted considerable attention due to their unique physicochemical properties and their promising applications in various fields of (bio)chemistry and (bio)analysis [1–4]. One of the novel classes of highly luminescent nanomaterials is carbon dots (C-dots), which were accidently discovered in 2004 during the purification of single-walled carbon nanotubes [5]. C-dots are generally oxygen-containing carbonaceous NPs with quasi-spherical shapes and sizes below 10nm. They typically display high quantum yield fluorescence with interesting size- and excitation wavelength-dependent behavior [6–8]. Although the origin of the fluorescence of C-dots is a matter of current debate, it is generally believed that their fluorescence is due to the radiative recombination of excitons located at surface energy traps and defects. C-dots can be synthesized by various methods, including simple and inexpensive solvothermal and microwave pyrolysis approaches. The synthesized C-dots typically contain many carboxylic acid moieties at their surfaces; therefore, they are water soluble and can be subsequently functionalized with various chemical groups [7,8]. The fascinating properties of this new class of emitters make them an interesting alternative to other luminescent nanomaterials. Especially, compared to semiconductor quantum dots (QDs) as popular luminescent NPs, C-dots have several important advantages including low toxicity, high chemical stability and low environmental hazard [9,10].

The superior emitting properties of C-dots attract the growing attention to applications of these nanomaterials in bioimaging [11–14], photocatalysis [15] and fluorescence [16–22]. However, there are only a few reports on the electrochemiluminescence (ECL) [22–24] and chemiluminescence (CL) studies of C-dots [25–28].

CL is the emission of light from an excited species produced by a chemical reaction. CL-based analytical methods show several advantages such as high sensitivity, simplicity of instrumentation, low detection limits, large calibration ranges and short analysis time [29]. Nowadays, due to the unique chemical and physical properties of NPs, the study of CL has been extended to NP-based systems from traditional molecular reactions. NPs can participate in CL reactions as catalyst, reductant, luminophore or energy acceptor [30,31]. The CL property of some nanomaterials as emitting species has been investigated [32–36], but lots of previous reports in the literature describes the catalytic effect of NPs on the CL reactions [37–43].

In the present work, direct CL of C-dots induced by K3Fe(CN)6 was investigated for the first time. The mechanism of C-dot CL system was proposed based on the fluorescence and CL emission spectra and the effects of radical scavengers on the CL intensity. Finally, the analytical application potential of this CL system was investigated.

The CL signals were monitored by LUMAT LB 9507 chemiluminometer (Berthold; www.berthold.com). CL spectrum was recorded by RF-540 spectrofluorimeter (Shimadzu, Japan) using flow mode with the excitation light source being turned off. The fluorescence spectra were also recorded by the same instrument under normal conditions. UV–Vis spectra were recorded on a Cary-100 Spectrophotometer (Varian; www.varianinc.com). The size and shape of C-dots were characterized by transmission electron microscopy (TEM, Leo 906, Zeiss, Germany).

All reagents were of analytical-reagent grade. Doubly distilled deionized water (obtained from Ghazi Serum Co., Tabriz, Iran) was used throughout the experiment. Sodium hydroxide (NaOH), potassium ferricyanide (K3Fe(CN)6), glycerin, citric acid monohydrate and poly(ethylene glycol) with average molecular weight of 1000 (PEG1000) were all purchased from Merck (Darmstadt, Germany).

C-dots were synthesized solvothermally according to the literature [25] with some modifications. Briefly, 15mL of glycerin, 1.0g of PEG1000 and 1.0g of citric acid monohydrate were mixed to become a homogeneous solution. The mixture was sealed into a 100mL Teflon equipped stainless steel autoclave and heated at 220°C for 12h in a muffle furnace. The solution was cooled down to ambient temperature naturally. Afterwards, in order to remove excess PEG1000 and glycerin, the prepared C-dots were purified by dialysis against deionized water for 24h (The cutoff of the dialysis membrane was equivalent to MW =2000). The concentration of the C-dot solution was calculated according to the concentration of carbon atom in the carbon source.

CL analyses were carried out in a 3mL tube, in the batch condition. An amount of 500μL of C-dots (0.57molL−1) and 150μL of NaOH (5molL−1) were added into the cell. Then an appropriate volume of sample or standard solution was added and the volume was reached to 900μL with distilled water. After injection of 100μL of 0.001molL−1 K3Fe(CN)6 by an automatic injector, monitoring of CL signal versus time was started automatically. Maximum CL intensity was used as analytical signal.

We used a simple solvothermal route for synthesis of C-dots using citric acid as a carbon source and PEG1000 as a passivation agent. According to the TEM image (Fig. 1 ), the synthesized C-dots were of spherical shape and monodisperse NPs with particle size distribution in the range of 5±2nm. The prepared C-dots exhibited a blue color under ultraviolet radiation (λ =365nm). Fig. 2 A shows the UV–Vis absorption spectrum of the C-dots. The fluorescence behavior of C-dots was also investigated. The maximum emission of ∼445nm was obtained with an excitation wavelength of 360nm. Moreover, the emission wavelength red shifted with increasing excitation wavelength, which revealed a distribution of the different surface energy traps of the C-dots (Fig. 2B). The fluorescence quantum yield of the prepared C-dots was found to be 8.6% by using quinine sulfate as a standard.

The preliminary studies indicated that a relatively strong CL emission was produced when C-dot solution was mixed with some oxidants in alkaline or acidic aqueous medium. Therefore, the reactions between C-dots and various concentrations of common oxidants applied in CL reactions (including K3Fe(CN)6, KMnO4, H2O2, Ce(IV) and NaIO4) were investigated. As shown in Fig. 3 , all of these oxidants are able to directly elicit a CL from C-dots, but the CL produced with K3Fe(CN)6 was much more intensive than that of the other oxidants. This may be due to the fact that the chemical energy generated during the redox reaction of C-dots with K3Fe(CN)6 is more matched with the excitation energy required for the formation the excited-state C-dots than the other oxidants. Thus K3Fe(CN)6 in alkaline aqueous solution was selected as the best oxidant for the CL reaction. Although the C-dots were purified by dialysis, a control experiment with the reagents used for the preparation of C-dots (the mixture of citric acid, PEG1000 and glycerin) was also carried out. Very weak CL was found for this solution. This confirms that the CL arises from C-dots.
The kinetic profiles of C-dot/K3Fe(CN)6 CL system (Fig. 4 ) showed that the CL reaction was very fast and the CL intensity reached a maximum value within 1s after injection of the oxidant solution.

In order to identify the emitting species in the CL reaction, the CL emission spectrum of C-dot/K3Fe(CN)6 system was obtained using a spectrofluorimeter in flow mode. The obtained CL spectrum (Fig. 5 A, curve a) indicated that there was an emission band in the range of 400–600nm with a maximum at ∼515nm. This CL spectrum is quite similar to the C-dot fluorescence spectrum with the excitation wavelength of 450nm (Fig. 5A, curve b). Therefore, the CL may be attributed to the excited C-dots. It should be mentioned that the fluorescence spectrum of C-dots in alkaline medium is the same as that in neutral medium, except its intensity is reduced. The fact that CL spectrum red-shifted compared to the most intense fluorescence spectrum (see Fig. 2B) may be explained by considering the difference in the emitting states of the CL and fluorescence emission. It is assumed that like ECL, the CL of C-dots is mainly produced by surface-state transitions (due to the presence of surface traps with incomplete passivation) whereas the fluorescence is more reminiscent of core state [7,26]. The energy band gap of surface states is smaller than that of the core states and therefore, the wavelength of CL emission is higher than that of fluorescence. The CL spectra for KMnO4/C-dot and H2O2/C-dot systems in alkaline conditions were also obtained. The same peak at about 515nm was obtained in both cases (Fig. 5A, inset). These results are in agreement with that reported earlier for Ce(IV)/C-dot system [28].

In order to confirm the reaction of C-dots with K3Fe(CN)6, the fluorescence spectrum of C-dots was recorded after mixing with this oxidant. According to the results, after addition of K3Fe(CN)6 to C-dot solution, the fluorescence peak of C-dots at 445nm decreased. So it can be concluded that the chemical reaction occurred between C-dots and K3Fe(CN)6. Furthermore, the UV–Vis absorption spectrum of K3Fe(CN)6 before and after addition of various amounts of C-dots was also recorded. As shown in Fig. 5B, the absorption peak of ferricyanide at about 420nm markedly decreased after reaction with C-dots. Based on these facts, the CL reaction can be described in its simplest form as follows:
C-dot + Fe
( CN )
6
3 -
→ C-
dot
∗
+ Fe
( CN )
6
4 -
C-
dot
∗
→ C-dot + hv
The excited-state C-dots can result from annihilation of electron-injected and hole-injected C-dots produced by a series of possible reactions. Ferricyanide can serve as the hole injector and convert C-dots to C-dot•+. On the other hand, superoxide anion radical formed by dissolved oxygen in strong alkaline medium in the presence of C-dots as a reducing agent may act as an electron injector to produce C-dot•−. Chemically generated exciton then is relaxed by electron–hole recombination to produces CL emission:
C-dot + Fe
( CN )
6
3 -
→ C-
dot
• +
+ Fe
( CN )
6
4 -
O
2
+
OH
-
→
H
2
O +
O
2
• -
C-dot +
O
2
• -
→ C-
dot
• -
+
O
2
C-
dot
• +
+ C-
dot
• -
→ C-
dot
∗
C-
dot
∗
→ C-dot + h ν ( ∼ 515
nm )
To further confirm this pathway, the effect of deoxygenation and some radical scavengers on the CL intensity of C-dots/K3Fe(CN)6 system was investigated. It was found that upon deoxygenation of solution by Ar bubbling, the CL intensity decreased by about 50%. Moreover, quercetin (0.001M) which is known to be a superoxide scavenger [44], had a remarkable negative effect on the CL intensity. Thus it was assumed that
O
2
-
is generated in the examined systems. Also, ascorbic acid as a common oxygen free radical scavenger [45] had a negative effect on the CL signal, which further confirmed that the generation of a free radical is critical in the CL reaction. Based on the above discussion, it can be concluded that C-dots are the main emitters in the CL system.

In order to investigate the effect of the solvothermal reaction time on the CL response of C-dots, the solutions of C-dot with different reaction times were prepared. As can be seen in Fig. 6 A, the CL intensity of C-dots increased with increasing the reaction time, and reached a constant value after 12h. It seems that with the increase of the reaction time, the surface defects of the C-dots continuously developed and resulted in an improvement in the emission efficiency [25,46]. After a certain time, the states of the C-dot surface were not changed significantly and the C-dots had more suitable surface structure for CL reaction. Therefore, the solution of C-dot prepared at 12h reaction time was selected for the next experiments.

The influence of C-dot concentration on the CL signal was investigated (Fig. 6B). It was found that the CL intensity linearly increased along with the increased concentration of C-dot in the range of 0.057–0.456molL−1 with a correlation coefficient of 0.993.

The effect of K3Fe(CN)6 concentration on the CL intensity over the range of 2×10−5 to 4×10−4 molL−1 was examined. According to the results (Fig. 7 A) the CL intensity of C-dots increased as the K3Fe(CN)6 concentration increased and reached a plateau after 10−4 molL−1. Therefore, this amount was selected as the optimum concentration.

Finally the influence of the NaOH concentration in the range of 0.05–1.5molL−1 was studied and the results are shown in Fig. 7B. The CL response was increased by increasing NaOH concentration up to 0.75molL−1, over which the relative CL intensity did not increase remarkably any more. So, 0.75molL−1 NaOH was chosen as the optimum value for consequent experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analytical application of the CL system
ce:section.ce:para:
In order to explore the analytical applicability of the C-dot/K3Fe(CN)6 CL system, the effect of several organic and inorganic species on the reaction was examined. The CL intensity may be changed in the presence of organic compounds containing hydroxyl, amino or mercapto groups due to the reaction of these reducing groups with the oxygen-containing intermediate radicals [47,48]. As previously mentioned, some intermediate radicals such as
O
2
-
are formed during the C-dot/K3Fe(CN)6 reaction. The reducing groups of organic compound are likely to compete with C-dots for active oxygen intermediates, leading to a decrease in CL intensity. Some inorganic cations may also inhibit the CL reaction through various interactions with the system such as oxidation of C-dots, complexing with C-dots or reaction with oxidant and radicals. The obtained results for several species are listed in Table 1 .

The corresponding calibration graphs for some species, which render the most efficient diminishing effects, are shown in Table 2 . These results indicate that the developed CL system has a good analytical potential for the detection of variety of compounds at trace levels. Moreover, combined with high performance liquid chromatography or capillary electrophoresis, the proposed CL system can be applied to the simultaneous analysis of numerous compounds. Furthermore, the nontoxicity and many unique properties of C-dots make this CL system applicable to immunoassay and bioanalysis research.

In order to evaluate the capability of the proposed method in real sample analysis, it was applied to the determination of Cr(VI) in various water samples. Recovery experiments were performed to evaluate the accuracy of this method. The obtained results were summarized in Table 3 . Statistical analysis of these results using Student t-test showed that there are no significant differences between added and found values.

In addition, the proposed method was applied to the determination of adrenaline in a pharmaceutical formulation (Adrenaline injection with nominal amount of 1.0mgmL−1 adrenaline). The obtained amount by the proposed method was 0.99±0.01, which is in excellent agreement with the labeled value.

In this work, we demonstrated that C-dots could be directly oxidized by K3Fe(CN)6 to give rise to a relatively strong CL emission. The mechanism of C-dot CL system was investigated based on the CL emission and fluorescence spectra, and the effects of radical scavengers on the CL intensity. According to the obtained results, C-dots could react with K3Fe(CN)6 and superoxide anion radical in alkaline solution to form C-dot•+ and C-dot•−. The electron-transfer annihilation of hole-injected and electron-injected particles forms the excited-state C-dot∗, which acts as the final emitter in the system. Moreover, an analytical method was developed for the determination of some biologically important molecules and metal ions that could inhibit the CL signals of C-dot/K3Fe(CN)6 system.
